site.title: ReduceCO2Now
site.tagline: Climate action, grounded in science — driven by people.
site.description: ReduceCO2Now — a global movement to stabilize Earth's climate. Science-based. Join now.
site.defaultDescription: ReduceCO2Now — an initiative to reduce CO₂ emissions. Join, learn, take action.

hero.subtitle: A clear warning — and a path to solutions. The science is urgent. The time is now.
hero.ppmLabel: Atmospheric CO₂ — Highest in 14 Million Years
hero.ppmContext: Stable below 300 ppm for 800,000 years · The spike is human-made

science.label: The Numbers
science.body: Every major climate indicator is moving in the wrong direction — measured, not projected.

charts.label: Interactive Data
charts.body: Historical data from ice cores and direct measurement. Future scenarios based on current emission trajectories.
charts.industrialSubtitle: Historical measurements from ice cores + Mauna Loa Observatory. Projections based on IPCC emission pathways.
charts.iceCoreTitle: 800,000 Years of CO₂ — The Deep Time View
charts.iceCoreSubtitle: Ice core data from Vostok & EPICA (Antarctica). Today's level is off the natural range by a wide margin.
charts.legendNote: Hover chart to see values · Click legend to toggle

predictor.label: Explore Scenarios
predictor.body: Drag the slider to explore what different CO₂ concentration levels mean for temperature and life on Earth.
predictor.footnote: Based on equilibrium climate sensitivity (~3°C per CO₂ doubling, IPCC AR6). Real warming depends on emission trajectories and feedbacks.

history.label: Deep Time Perspective
history.body: Earth's climate record spans millions of years. CO₂ has always been the primary lever. We're pushing it faster than anything in the geological record.
history.header: CO₂ Through Time — Click a Point to Explore

warning.label: Consequences
warning.bannerDesc: These aren't distant projections. Climate change is reshaping weather systems, ocean currents, agricultural zones, and habitable land right now. Every fraction of a degree amplifies these effects.

solutions.label: What We Can Do
solutions.body: Stabilizing CO₂ is possible — but requires coordinated action at scale. These are proven levers. We can pull all of them simultaneously.

discord.desc: Meet us in our daily meetings on Discord. Connect with scientists, activists, and concerned citizens from around the world. Find out all about the project, events, and how you can make a real difference.

newsletter.label: Newsletter
newsletter.body: Subscribe for science-based facts, global solutions, and action opportunities delivered to your inbox.
newsletter.consent: I consent to my email address being processed for the purpose of sending the newsletter. Consent may be withdrawn at any time.
newsletter.noteRespect: We respect your privacy. Unsubscribe anytime.
newsletter.notePlaceholder: Note: this form is currently a placeholder and does not yet send messages.

community.label: Global Community
community.body: Our movement spans 25+ languages. Find your community on your platform.

footer.tagline: A global movement to stabilize Earth's climate — grounded in science, driven by people.

co2Today.title: Atmospheric CO₂ Today
co2Today.desc: Stable below 300 ppm for 800,000 years. The human-driven spike is unprecedented in geological history.

tempRise.title: Above Pre-Industrial Levels
tempRise.desc: 2024 was the hottest year ever recorded. Oceans store heat — what we see now is only the beginning.

stableClimate.title: Of Stable Climate, Now Gone
stableClimate.desc: Ice core data shows CO₂ oscillated between 170–300 ppm through dozens of ice age cycles — until the industrial era.

co2History.title: Since CO₂ Was This High
co2History.desc: The last time CO₂ reached today's levels, Earth was 4°C hotter — no polar ice, sea levels dramatically higher.

warming2100.title: Warming at 800 ppm by 2100
warming2100.desc: A climate not seen in 34 million years. Unlimited fossil fuel use could recreate conditions from 50 million years ago.

co2IceAges.title: CO₂ During Ice Ages
co2IceAges.desc: Ice ages saw CO₂ fall to ~170 ppm and temperatures drop 10°C. CO₂ is the planet's primary temperature dial.

ticker.lastTime: Since CO₂ was last this high
ticker.foodWasted: Of all food produced is wasted

solutions.label: What We Can Do
solutions.body: Stabilizing CO₂ is possible — but requires coordinated action at scale. These are proven levers. We can pull all of them simultaneously.

energy.title: Reduce Fossil Fuel Use
energy.desc: Transition to renewables, increase carbon pricing, and shift incentives away from oil, gas and coal. The single largest lever available.

carbon.title: Bury Biomass & Capture Carbon
carbon.desc: Biochar, enhanced weathering, and direct air capture can actively remove CO₂ already in the atmosphere — not just slow new emissions.

food.title: Eat a CO₂-Optimized Diet
food.desc: Food production accounts for ~25% of global emissions. Shifting to plant-rich diets and choosing local foods makes a measurable difference.

waste.title: Reduce Food Waste
waste.desc: ~1/3 of all food produced globally is wasted, creating a massive hidden carbon footprint. Cutting waste is high-impact and immediate.

consumption.title: Cut Overconsumption
consumption.desc: Manufacturing, shipping and disposing of goods drives significant emissions. Buying less, buying smarter, extending product lifetimes all help.

policy.title: Carbon Pricing & Policy
policy.desc: Make fossil fuels reflect their true cost. Carbon taxes and cap-and-trade systems have proven track records in reducing emissions at scale.

predictor.label: Explore Scenarios
predictor.body: Drag the slider to explore what different CO₂ concentration levels mean for temperature and life on Earth.
predictor.footnote: Based on equilibrium climate sensitivity (~3°C per CO₂ doubling, IPCC AR6). Real warming depends on emission trajectories and feedbacks.

1990s: Arctic warming faster than global average
today: 1 billion people at higher flood risk
moderate: Major crop yield declines in tropics
bau: Annual extreme heat events become norm
extreme: Climate not seen in 34 million years

title.miocene: Last Time CO₂ Was This High

desc.natural: CO₂ oscillated naturally between 180–300 ppm for 800,000 years, in sync with Earth's orbital cycles. Ice ages plunged temperatures 10°C; warm interglacials rose to ~280 ppm. No human civilization existed.
desc.miocene: Earth was 4°C warmer globally. No polar ice sheets as we know them. Sea levels were 20–40 meters higher. The continents looked similar but the climate was radically different — no humans, no modern ecosystems.
desc.ignition: Coal, then oil and gas began releasing carbon stored over millions of years. CO₂ rose slowly at first. By 1950 we crossed 310 ppm — still within range of natural variation, but the trajectory was set.
desc.accel: The Keeling Curve shows an unmistakable upward trend from 1958. Global GDP growth, population explosion, and fossil fuel dependency compound. Each decade adds more CO₂ than the last.
desc.now: We've passed the 1.5°C threshold. The rise is 100× faster than any natural transition in the geological record. The last time Earth saw this CO₂, humans didn't exist. We are the experiment.
